1â†’---
     2â†’name: late-media
     3â†’description: "Late.dev media upload workflow with presigned URLs, validation, and platform-specific constraints"
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Late.dev Media Upload Workflow
     7â†’
     8â†’## Upload Flow
     9â†’
    10â†’1. **Validate** file exists, check type/size against platform limits
    11â†’2. **Validate content-type** matches actual file magic bytes (security)
    12â†’3. **Get presigned URL**: `late-tool.mjs media presign --filename=photo.jpg --content-type=image/jpeg`
    13â†’4. **Upload** file to presigned URL via PUT (handled internally)
    14â†’5. **Reference** `publicUrl` in post's `mediaItems` array
    15â†’
    16â†’```bash
    17â†’# Step 1: Get presigned URL
    18â†’node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/late-tool.mjs media presign \
    19â†’  --filename=photo.jpg --content-type=image/jpeg
    20â†’# Returns: { "publicUrl": "https://...", "uploadUrl": "https://..." }
    21â†’# Note: uploadUrl is internal only â€” never log it
    22â†’
    23â†’# Step 2: Reference in post
    24â†’node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/late-tool.mjs posts create \
    25â†’  --accounts='["acc_123"]' \
    26â†’  --text="Check this out!" \
    27â†’  --media='[{"publicUrl":"https://...","type":"image"}]'
    28â†’```
    29â†’
    30â†’## Platform Constraints
    31â†’
    32â†’| Platform | Images | Video | Other |
    33â†’|----------|--------|-------|-------|
    34â†’| LinkedIn | JPEG/PNG/GIF (multiple) | MP4/MOV | PDF docs (100MB, 300pp max) |
    35â†’| X/Twitter | 4 images max OR 1 video | MP4/MOV | â€” |
    36â†’
    37â†’## Security
    38â†’
    39â†’- Presigned URLs are bearer tokens â€” **never persist to logs or DB**
    40â†’- Use immediately after creation (TTL ~15 minutes, Late.dev-controlled). If the API allows configuring TTL, request 5 minutes maximum to minimize exposure window
    41â†’- Validate content-type matches file magic bytes before upload
    42â†’- Max 5GB per file
    43â†’

1â†’---
     2â†’name: media-handler-agent
     3â†’description: Uploads, resizes, and validates media per platform limits
     4â†’model: inherit
     5â†’color: yellow
     6â†’tools: ["Read", "Bash"]
     7â†’---
     8â†’
     9â†’# Media Handler Agent
    10â†’
    11â†’## Core Responsibilities
    12â†’
    13â†’1. Read `late-media` skill for upload workflow
    14â†’2. Validate file types and sizes per platform constraints
    15â†’3. Validate content-type matches actual file magic bytes (security)
    16â†’4. Get presigned URLs via `late-tool.mjs media presign`
    17â†’5. Upload files to presigned URLs
    18â†’6. Return public URLs mapped to each platform's post
    19â†’
    20â†’## Input Schema
    21â†’
    22â†’```json
    23â†’{
    24â†’  "mediaPaths": ["/path/to/photo.jpg", "/path/to/video.mp4"],
    25â†’  "targetPlatforms": ["linkedin", "x"],
    26â†’  "variants": [ "...from preview agent..." ]
    27â†’}
    28â†’```
    29â†’
    30â†’## Processing Steps
    31â†’
    32â†’For each media file:
    33â†’
    34â†’1. **Validate file exists**: Check file is accessible
    35â†’2. **Check file type**: Verify against platform constraints
    36â†’   - LinkedIn: JPEG/PNG/GIF, MP4/MOV, PDF (100MB, 300pp)
    37â†’   - X: 4 images max OR 1 video, MP4/MOV
    38â†’3. **Validate content-type**: Match MIME type to file magic bytes
    39â†’4. **Get presigned URL**:
    40â†’   ```bash
    41â†’   node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/late-tool.mjs media presign \
    42â†’     --filename=<filename> --content-type=<mime-type>
    43â†’   ```
    44â†’5. **Upload**: PUT to presigned URL (internal)
    45â†’6. **Collect**: Map public URLs to platform variants
    46â†’
    47â†’## Security
    48â†’
    49â†’- Presigned URLs are bearer tokens â€” never log them
    50â†’- Use immediately after creation (15-minute TTL)
    51â†’- Validate content-type matches file magic bytes before upload
    52â†’- Max 5GB per file
    53â†’
    54â†’## Output Schema
    55â†’
    56â†’```json
    57â†’{
    58â†’  "status": "complete",
    59â†’  "mediaMap": {
    60â†’    "linkedin": [
    61â†’      { "publicUrl": "https://...", "type": "image", "filename": "photo.jpg" }
    62â†’    ],
    63â†’    "x": [
    64â†’      { "publicUrl": "https://...", "type": "image", "filename": "photo.jpg" }
    65â†’    ]
    66â†’  }
    67â†’}
    68â†’```
    69â†’
    70â†’## Error Handling
    71â†’
    72â†’- File not found: report error, skip file, continue with remaining
    73â†’- Invalid file type for platform: report warning, skip for that platform
    74â†’- Upload failure: report error with `LATE_MEDIA_ERROR` code
    75â†’- If no media files: pass through with empty mediaMap (not an error)
    76â†’

1â†’---
     2â†’name: Morning Briefing
     3â†’description: "Gathers overnight data from Gmail, Calendar, Notion, Slack, and Drive into a consolidated morning update. Activates when the user wants a morning sync, overnight catch-up, or asks 'what happened while I was away?' Handles multi-source data gathering with overnight window calculation and graceful degradation when sources are unavailable."
     4â†’globs:
     5â†’  - "commands/morning-sync.md"
     6â†’  - "commands/morning-quick.md"
     7â†’---
     8â†’
     9â†’## Overview
    10â†’
    11â†’Gather overnight data from five sources -- Gmail, Google Calendar, Notion, Slack, and Google Drive -- into a single structured dataset for priority synthesis and briefing assembly. Operate as an orchestrator that directly calls gws CLI and MCP tools rather than delegating to agent teams. Reuse domain knowledge from established plugins: P02 Daily Briefing Generator for email prioritization and calendar scoring, P19 Slack Digest Engine for message noise filtering and signal scoring, and P18 Google Drive Brain for file search and relevance ranking. This skill handles data collection and normalization only -- priority ranking, cross-source correlation, and final briefing formatting belong to the priority-synthesis skill and command layer respectively.
    12â†’
    13â†’## Overnight Window Calculation
    14â†’
    15â†’Calculate the overnight window before initiating any source queries. All five sources use the same cutoff timestamp to ensure consistent time boundaries across the briefing.
    16â†’
    17â†’### Default Window
    18â†’
    19â†’Set the default overnight window to 12 hours back from the current time. When the current time is 7:00 AM, the window covers from 7:00 PM the previous evening through the present moment.
    20â†’
    21â†’### Configurable Window
    22â†’
    23â†’Accept a `--since` flag to override the default. Parse the following formats:
    24â†’
    25â†’| Format | Example | Resolution |
    26â†’|--------|---------|------------|
    27â†’| Hours suffix | `8h`, `24h` | Current time minus N hours |
    28â†’| ISO datetime | `2026-03-04T22:00:00` | Exact cutoff timestamp |
    29â†’
    30â†’When the `--since` value is unparseable, fall back to the 12-hour default and append a warning: "Could not parse --since value '[input]', using default 12-hour window."
    31â†’
    32â†’### Cutoff Timestamp
    33â†’
    34â†’Store the resolved cutoff as an ISO 8601 datetime. Pass this timestamp to every source query. Record both the cutoff datetime and the window duration in the output metadata for downstream consumption.
    35â†’
    36â†’## Five-Source Gathering Pipeline
    37â†’
    38â†’Process sources in the following fixed order. Complete each source before starting the next. Record timing and status for every source regardless of outcome.
    39â†’
    40â†’### Source 1: Gmail (Required)
    41â†’
    42â†’Scan unread emails received within the overnight window. Construct a Gmail search query using `is:unread after:{cutoff_date}` where `cutoff_date` is the overnight window cutoff formatted as `YYYY/MM/DD`.
    43â†’
    44â†’Apply P02's email-prioritization patterns to classify each unread email using the Eisenhower matrix (Q1: Urgent and Important, Q2: Important, Q3: Urgent, Q4: Neither). Use sender tier classification, subject keyword signals, action-required flag detection, and recency weighting as defined in P02's email-prioritization skill.
    45â†’
    46â†’Collapse threads to a single entry using the latest message per thread. Extract up to 10 highlights ordered by quadrant then sender tier then recency.
    47â†’
    48â†’For each highlight, extract: sender (display name and email), subject (truncated at 80 characters), summary (100 characters max), quadrant assignment, action needed (boolean with action phrase), and recency (relative timestamp).
    49â†’
    50â†’Return:
    51â†’- `email_count`: Total unread emails in the overnight window.
    52â†’- `highlights`: List of up to 10 structured highlight records.
    53â†’- `unread_total`: Total unread count across all time (not just the window).
    54â†’- `status`: "available", "unavailable", or "partial".
    55â†’
    56â†’When gws CLI is unavailable for Gmail, set status to "unavailable", all counts to 0, highlights to an empty list, and continue to the next source.
    57â†’
    58â†’### Source 2: Google Calendar (Required)
    59â†’
    60â†’Fetch all events for today (start of day 00:00 to end of day 23:59 in the user's local timezone).
    61â†’
    62â†’Filter out events matching any of these criteria:
    63â†’- RSVP status is "declined".
    64â†’- Event status is "cancelled".
    65â†’- Title contains "Focus", "Block", or "OOO" (case-insensitive).
    66â†’- Event has zero attendees (personal reminders).
    67â†’
    68â†’Classify each remaining event by meeting type using this priority order (first match wins):
    69â†’1. **external-client**: At least one attendee domain differs from the user's domain AND the event title or attendees match known client contacts.
    70â†’2. **one-on-one**: Exactly two attendees (user + one other).
    71â†’3. **ad-hoc**: Non-recurring event with 3+ attendees.
    72â†’4. **recurring**: Event has a recurrence rule.
    73â†’5. **group-meeting**: 5+ attendees, non-recurring.
    74â†’6. **internal-sync**: Default fallback for all other events.
    75â†’
    76â†’Score importance on a 1-5 scale using four weighted factors:
    77â†’- Attendee seniority (0.30): Higher when attendees include leadership or C-suite titles.
    78â†’- External vs internal (0.25): External attendees score higher.
    79â†’- New vs recurring (0.25): First-time meetings score higher than recurring.
    80â†’- Deal proximity (0.20): Higher when attendees or title match active deals or proposals.
    81â†’
    82â†’For each event, extract: title, start time, duration in minutes, attendee count, meeting type, and importance score.
    83â†’
    84â†’Return:
    85â†’- `meeting_count`: Number of qualifying events.
    86â†’- `events`: List of structured event records sorted by start time.
    87â†’- `status`: "available", "unavailable", or "partial".
    88â†’
    89â†’### Source 3: Notion (Required)
    90â†’
    91â†’Search for task databases in the user's Notion workspace. Identify databases containing both a Status property and a Date property (Due Date, Deadline, or similar). Use the same auto-discovery algorithm as P05 Weekly Review Compiler -- search all accessible databases and qualify by the presence of completion-like status values.
    92â†’
    93â†’Query discovered databases for tasks matching either condition:
    94â†’- Due Date equals today.
    95â†’- Due Date is before today AND Status is not "Done", "Completed", or "Archived" (overdue items).
    96â†’
    97â†’Additionally, search for tasks where Status changed to "Done" or "Completed" within the overnight window to surface overnight completions.
    98â†’
    99â†’For each task, extract: task title, current status, due date, project or parent database name, and priority property if available.
   100â†’
   101â†’Return:
   102â†’- `task_count`: Total tasks due today plus overdue.
   103â†’- `items`: List of structured task records.
   104â†’- `overdue`: Count of overdue items specifically.
   105â†’- `status`: "available", "unavailable", or "partial".
   106â†’
   107â†’### Source 4: Slack (Optional)
   108â†’
   109â†’If Slack MCP is available, scan channels for unread messages posted within the overnight window. List joined channels and filter to configured channels if a channel list is specified, otherwise scan all joined channels.
   110â†’
   111â†’Apply P19's noise filtering pipeline in order: channel management messages, automated bot messages, social and off-topic content, repeated and duplicate messages, link dumps without context, low-signal acknowledgments. Discard messages matching any noise category unless they contain a direct @mention of the current user.
   112â†’
   113â†’For surviving messages, compute P19's 4-factor signal score: message type (0-40) + engagement (0-25) + recency (0-20) + channel importance (-5 to +15). Surface only messages scoring P1 (75-100) or P2 (50-74), plus all direct @mentions regardless of score.
   114â†’
   115â†’For each surfaced message, extract: channel name, sender display name, message preview (truncated at 100 characters), signal score, and is_mention boolean.
   116â†’
   117â†’Return:
   118â†’- `slack_highlights`: Count of surfaced messages.
   119â†’- `items`: List of structured message records.
   120â†’- `status`: "available" or "unavailable".
   121â†’
   122â†’If Slack MCP is unavailable, return status "unavailable", slack_highlights as 0, items as an empty list. Do not log a warning or error -- optional source unavailability is normal.
   123â†’
   124â†’### Source 5: Google Drive (Optional)
   125â†’
   126â†’If Google gws CLI is available for Drive, search for files modified after the overnight window cutoff. Construct a Drive search query filtering by `modifiedTime > '{cutoff_iso}'`.
   127â†’
   128â†’Filter results:
   129â†’- Skip files owned by the current user that have no external edits, comments, or shares within the window.
   130â†’- Surface files that were shared with the user during the window.
   131â†’- Surface files with new comments added during the window.
   132â†’- Surface files created during the window by other users in shared folders.
   133â†’
   134â†’For each qualifying file, extract: file name, file type (Docs/Sheets/Slides/PDF/other), last modifier name, modified timestamp, and shared-with list.
   135â†’
   136â†’Return:
   137â†’- `drive_updates`: Count of qualifying file updates.
   138â†’- `items`: List of structured file records.
   139â†’- `status`: "available" or "unavailable".
   140â†’
   141â†’If Google gws CLI is unavailable for Drive, return status "unavailable", drive_updates as 0, items as an empty list. Do not log a warning or error.
   142â†’
   143â†’## Source Status Reporting
   144â†’
   145â†’Track the status of each source throughout the gathering pipeline. Assign one of three statuses per source:
   146â†’
   147â†’| Status | Meaning |
   148â†’|--------|---------|
   149â†’| available | MCP server connected, data retrieved successfully |
   150â†’| unavailable | gws CLI unavailable or authentication not configured or connection failed |
   151â†’| partial | MCP server connected but returned incomplete data (timeout, rate limit, API error) |
   152â†’
   153â†’### Required vs Optional Source Handling
   154â†’
   155â†’**Required sources** (Gmail, Calendar, Notion): Log a warning when any required source returns "unavailable" but continue the pipeline. Include the warning in the output metadata so the command layer can surface it to the user.
   156â†’
   157â†’**Optional sources** (Slack, Drive): Silently set counts to 0 and status to "unavailable". Do not log warnings. Omit the corresponding section entirely from the final briefing -- do not render an empty section or a "Slack unavailable" placeholder.
   158â†’
   159â†’### Sources Used List
   160â†’
   161â†’Compile a `sources_used` list containing only sources with status "available" or "partial". Use this list for Notion DB metadata when logging the briefing.
   162â†’
   163â†’## Graceful Degradation Rules
   164â†’
   165â†’Determine the briefing tier based on the number of available required sources:
   166â†’
   167â†’| Available Required Sources | Briefing Tier | Behavior |
   168â†’|---------------------------|---------------|----------|
   169â†’| 3 (Gmail + Calendar + Notion) | Full briefing | Include all available optional sources |
   170â†’| 2 of 3 required | Partial briefing | Proceed with warning identifying the missing required source |
   171â†’| 1 of 3 required | Minimal briefing | Proceed with prominent warning, recommend configuring missing sources |
   172â†’| 0 required | Abort | Return actionable error listing which MCP servers to configure with package names |
   173â†’
   174â†’The abort error message must include specific MCP server package names:
   175â†’- Gmail: `gws` CLI (Gmail)
   176â†’- Google Calendar: `gws` CLI (Calendar)
   177â†’- Notion: `@modelcontextprotocol/server-notion`
   178â†’
   179â†’Never error or abort due to optional source unavailability. A briefing with zero optional sources and all three required sources is a full briefing.
   180â†’
   181â†’## Output Contract
   182â†’
   183â†’Produce a structured `gathered_data` object consumed by the priority-synthesis skill. Maintain this exact shape regardless of which sources are available -- unavailable sources return zero counts and empty lists.
   184â†’
   185â†’```
   186â†’gathered_data:
   187â†’  overnight_window:
   188â†’    since: [ISO 8601 cutoff datetime]
   189â†’    hours: [window duration as number]
   190â†’  sources:
   191â†’    gmail: [available | unavailable | partial]
   192â†’    calendar: [available | unavailable | partial]
   193â†’    notion: [available | unavailable | partial]
   194â†’    slack: [available | unavailable | partial]
   195â†’    drive: [available | unavailable | partial]
   196â†’  sources_used: [list of source names with status available or partial]
   197â†’  briefing_tier: [full | partial | minimal]
   198â†’  email:
   199â†’    count: [N]
   200â†’    highlights: [list of highlight records]
   201â†’    unread_total: [N]
   202â†’  calendar:
   203â†’    count: [N]
   204â†’    events: [list of event records]
   205â†’  tasks:
   206â†’    count: [N]
   207â†’    items: [list of task records]
   208â†’    overdue: [N]
   209â†’  slack:
   210â†’    highlights: [N]
   211â†’    items: [list of message records]
   212â†’  drive:
   213â†’    updates: [N]
   214â†’    items: [list of file records]
   215â†’```
   216â†’
   217â†’Pass this object directly to the priority-synthesis skill without modification. The priority-synthesis skill consumes the gathered data, applies cross-source correlation and priority ranking, and produces the formatted briefing.
   218â†’
   219â†’## Company Relation
   220â†’
   221â†’Populate the Company relation when the briefing is dominated by one client's emails/meetings. After gathering data from all sources, examine email sender domains and calendar meeting attendees. If 50% or more of the summarized items (email highlights + calendar events) relate to a single client, search the "[FOS] Companies" database (fall back to "Founder OS HQ - Companies") by domain match from email addresses or meeting attendee domains. If a matching Company is found, include its page ID in the output so the command layer can set the Company relation on the Briefings row.
   222â†’
   223â†’## Reference
   224â†’
   225â†’For detailed per-source query construction patterns, extraction field specifications, filtering rules, and edge case handling, consult:
   226â†’`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/morning/morning-briefing/references/source-patterns.md`
   227â†’

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me create a detailed report of my findings.

## Exploration Report: Founder OS Workspace Structure & Patterns

I've thoroughly explored the founder-workspace project. Here's what I found:

### 1. **Skill Structure Pattern**

Skills live in `development/skills/<namespace>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` and follow this format:

```yaml
---
name: [skill name]
description: "[When this skill activates, what it does]"
globs: ["commands/social/...", "..."]  # Which commands use this skill
---
```

**Key characteristics:**
- Skills are domain knowledge documents (not code), written in Markdown
- Each skill is 50-300 lines of detailed instructions
- Skills define algorithms, patterns, and step-by-step processes
- Multiple skills are read by a single command before execution
- Examples seen: `template-engine` (templating logic), `knowledge-retrieval` (search pipeline), `action-extraction` (email parsing), `cross-posting` (multi-platform adaptation), `posting-cadence` (scheduling patterns)

### 2. **Command Structure Pattern**

Commands live in `development/commands/<namespace>/<action>.md` and follow this format:

```yaml
---
description: [Human-readable description]
argument-hint: "[flags and args the command accepts]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash"]
execution-mode: background|foreground
result-format: summary|full
---
```

**Key characteristics:**
- Commands orchestrate skills and execution
- They include preflight checks, business context loading, memory injection, intelligence patterns
- Most are "background" (delegated as subagents via Task tool)
- Example flow (from `ideate:draft`):
  1. Parse arguments
  2. Check business context
  3. Run preflight check
  4. Load memories
  5. Apply learned patterns
  6. Execute 3 phases (Ideation â†’ Draft â†’ Validate)
  7. Log observations to memory/intelligence

### 3. **Content-Related Namespaces Found**

- **`social/`**: Posts, cross-posting, templates, cadence, composition (main social namespace)
- **`ideate/`**: Content drafting, frameworks, hooks, voice (feeds into social)
- **`newsletter/`**: Newsletter engine (part of content pillar)
- **`content/`**: Likely stored in Notion HQ as a merged database with "Social Post", "Newsletter", "LinkedIn Post" type values

Integration pattern: `ideate:draft` â†’ outputs content brief â†’ fed to `social:compose` (template-based) or `social:post` (publish)

### 4. **Late.dev Integration (Existing Video/Media Handling)**

Found pre-built infrastructure:
- **Skill**: `_infrastructure/late-skills/late-media/SKILL.md` â€” media upload workflow with presigned URLs
- **Agent**: `agents/social/media-handler-agent.md` â€” validates, resizes, uploads per platform limits
- **Constraints documented**: LinkedIn (JPEG/PNG/GIF/MP4/MOV), X/Twitter (4 images OR 1 video)
- Uses `late-tool.mjs media presign` for uploading

This is a pattern to follow for video integration.

### 5. **Spec Stub at [20]**

`docs/superpowers/specs/[20]-2026-03-18-remotion-skill-design.md` contains:

```
# [20] Remotion Skill Design

Status: Stub â€” pending specification

Topics to Specify:
- Remotion project scaffolding and dependency management
- Video template library (intro, outro, testimonial, product demo, explainer)
- Input format (text script, images, audio, brand assets)
- Composition API usage (scenes, transitions, timing)
- Text-to-speech integration options
- Brand kit integration (colors, fonts, logos)
- Rendering pipeline (local vs cloud render)
- Output formats and resolution presets (9:16, 16:9, 1:1)
- Preview workflow (still frames before full render)
- Asset management (media files, fonts, music)
- Skill command structure (`/founder-os:video:*`)
- Integration with content namespace (P24, P08)
```

### 6. **Key Infrastructure Patterns** (All Commands Use)

Every command shares this structure:
1. **Business Context Loading**: Check `_infrastructure/context/active/` for `.md` files
2. **Preflight Checks**: Validate required/optional external tools
3. **Memory Injection** (Step 0): Query memory store, inject top 5 relevant memories
4. **Intelligence Patterns**: Check for learned patterns from prior runs
5. **Multi-Phase Execution**: Display progress ("Phase 1/N", "Phase 2/N")
6. **Observation Logging**: Record pre_command and post_command events
7. **Self-Healing**: Classify errors and apply recovery strategies
8. **Final Memory Update**: Log observations to detect emerging patterns

### 7. **Package & Distribution Structure**

- **Source**: `development/` (50+ skills, 35+ commands, 7 agent teams)
- **Distribution**: `dist/` with compiled `package.json` v1.2.0
- **Scripts**: `development/scripts/package.json` (just `better-sqlite3` for local DB)

### 8. **Integration Points for Video Skill**

Based on patterns found:

**Namespace**: `video` (new)

**Commands** that would integrate video:
- `/founder-os:video:generate` â€” create video from script + assets
- `/founder-os:video:render` â€” execute rendering pipeline
- `/founder-os:video:template` â€” list/customize templates

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me create a detailed report of my findings.

## Exploration Report: Founder OS Workspace Structure & Patterns

I've thoroughly explored the founder-workspace project. Here's what I found:

### 1. **Skill Structure Pattern**

Skills live in `development/skills/<namespace>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` and follow this format:

```yaml
---
name: [skill name]
description: "[When this skill activates, what it does]"
globs: ["commands/social/...", "..."]  # Which commands use this skill
---
```

**Key characteristics:**
- Skills are domain knowledge documents (not code), written in Markdown
- Each skill is 50-300 lines of detailed instructions
- Skills define algorithms, patterns, and step-by-step processes
- Multiple skills are read by a single command before execution
- Examples seen: `template-engine` (templating logic), `knowledge-retrieval` (search pipeline), `action-extraction` (email parsing), `cross-posting` (multi-platform adaptation), `posting-cadence` (scheduling patterns)

### 2. **Command Structure Pattern**

Commands live in `development/commands/<namespace>/<action>.md` and follow this format:

```yaml
---
description: [Human-readable description]
argument-hint: "[flags and args the command accepts]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash"]
execution-mode: background|foreground
result-format: summary|full
---
```

**Key characteristics:**
- Commands orchestrate skills and execution
- They include preflight checks, business context loading, memory injection, intelligence patterns
- Most are "background" (delegated as subagents via Task tool)
- Example flow (from `ideate:draft`):
  1. Parse arguments
  2. Check business context
  3. Run preflight check
  4. Load memories
  5. Apply learned patterns
  6. Execute 3 phases (Ideation â†’ Draft â†’ Validate)
  7. Log observations to memory/intelligence

### 3. **Content-Related Namespaces Found**

- **`social/`**: Posts, cross-posting, templates, cadence, composition (main social namespace)
- **`ideate/`**: Content drafting, frameworks, hooks, voice (feeds into social)
- **`newsletter/`**: Newsletter engine (part of content pillar)
- **`content/`**: Likely stored in Notion HQ as a merged database with "Social Post", "Newsletter", "LinkedIn Post" type values

Integration pattern: `ideate:draft` â†’ outputs content brief â†’ fed to `social:compose` (template-based) or `social:post` (publish)

### 4. **Late.dev Integration (Existing Video/Media Handling)**

Found pre-built infrastructure:
- **Skill**: `_infrastructure/late-skills/late-media/SKILL.md` â€” media upload workflow with presigned URLs
- **Agent**: `agents/social/media-handler-agent.md` â€” validates, resizes, uploads per platform limits
- **Constraints documented**: LinkedIn (JPEG/PNG/GIF/MP4/MOV), X/Twitter (4 images OR 1 video)
- Uses `late-tool.mjs media presign` for uploading

This is a pattern to follow for video integration.

### 5. **Spec Stub at [20]**

`docs/superpowers/specs/[20]-2026-03-18-remotion-skill-design.md` contains:

```
# [20] Remotion Skill Design

Status: Stub â€” pending specification

Topics to Specify:
- Remotion project scaffolding and dependency management
- Video template library (intro, outro, testimonial, product demo, explainer)
- Input format (text script, images, audio, brand assets)
- Composition API usage (scenes, transitions, timing)
- Text-to-speech integration options
- Brand kit integration (colors, fonts, logos)
- Rendering pipeline (local vs cloud render)
- Output formats and resolution presets (9:16, 16:9, 1:1)
- Preview workflow (still frames before full render)
- Asset management (media files, fonts, music)
- Skill command structure (`/founder-os:video:*`)
- Integration with content namespace (P24, P08)
```

### 6. **Key Infrastructure Patterns** (All Commands Use)

Every command shares this structure:
1. **Business Context Loading**: Check `_infrastructure/context/active/` for `.md` files
2. **Preflight Checks**: Validate required/optional external tools
3. **Memory Injection** (Step 0): Query memory store, inject top 5 relevant memories
4. **Intelligence Patterns**: Check for learned patterns from prior runs
5. **Multi-Phase Execution**: Display progress ("Phase 1/N", "Phase 2/N")
6. **Observation Logging**: Record pre_command and post_command events
7. **Self-Healing**: Classify errors and apply recovery strategies
8. **Final Memory Update**: Log observations to detect emerging patterns

### 7. **Package & Distribution Structure**

- **Source**: `development/` (50+ skills, 35+ commands, 7 agent teams)
- **Distribution**: `dist/` with compiled `package.json` v1.2.0
- **Scripts**: `development/scripts/package.json` (just `better-sqlite3` for local DB)

### 8. **Integration Points for Video Skill**

Based on patterns found:

**Namespace**: `video` (new)

**Commands** that would integrate video:
- `/founder-os:video:generate` â€” create video from script + assets
- `/founder-os:video:render` â€” execute rendering pipeline

### 9. **Key Differences from Standard Code**

- **No JavaScript/TypeScript files** in the skill/command layer â€” all domain knowledge is Markdown
- **Heavy use of `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/` path references** for portability
- **Skills are read by commands, not invoked** â€” they're knowledge documents
- **Bash scripts** (like `late-tool.mjs`, `notion-tool.mjs`) handle actual integrations

Explain how Remotion rendering works: the renderMedia API, render options (format, codec, resolution), local vs cloud rendering, and the CLI render command. What are the key parameters and workflow?

content.content:
# Remotion's Fundamental Concepts

## Component-Based Video Creation

Remotion's core model treats video generation as a React programming problem. You build videos by creating React components that render different content based on the current frame number, enabling animations through frame-by-frame changes.

## The Frame-Based Programming Model

The framework provides a frame counter via the `useCurrentFrame()` hook. As stated in the documentation, "A video is a function of images over time. If you change content every frame, you'll end up with an animation." This means your components receive frame information and can conditionally render content accordingly.

## Video Configuration Properties

Every video has four essential properties accessible through `useVideoConfig()`:
- Dimensions (width and height in pixels)
- Duration (`durationInFrames`)
- Playback speed (`fps`)

Important timing detail: "A video's first frame is `0` and its last frame is `durationInFrames - 1`."

## The Composition System

Compositions are metadata-component pairs registered in your root file. By wrapping a React component with the `<Composition>` element in `src/Root.tsx`, you define renderable videos with their properties (ID, duration, frame rate, dimensions) and make them available in the editor interface.

## Rendering Trigger

Rendering is implicitly triggered by frame changes. When the frame number updates, React re-renders your component with the new frame value, automatically producing the next image in your video sequence.

# Remotion's Fundamental Concepts

## Component-Based Video Creation

Remotion's core model treats video generation as a React programming problem. You build videos by creating React components that render different content based on the current frame number, enabling animations through frame-by-frame changes.

## The Frame-Based Programming Model

The framework provides a frame counter via the `useCurrentFrame()` hook. As stated in the documentation, "A video is a function of images over time. If you change content every frame, you'll end up with an animation." This means your components receive frame information and can conditionally render content accordingly.

## Video Configuration Properties

Every video has four essential properties accessible through `useVideoConfig()`:
- Dimensions (width and height in pixels)
- Duration (`durationInFrames`)
- Playback speed (`fps`)

Important timing detail: "A video's first frame is `0` and its last frame is `durationInFrames - 1`."

## The Composition System

Compositions are metadata-component pairs registered in your root file. By wrapping a React component with the `<Composition>` element in `src/Root.tsx`, you define renderable videos with their properties (ID, duration, frame rate, dimensions) and make them available in the editor interface.

## Rendering Trigger

Rendering is implicitly triggered by frame changes. When the frame number updates, React re-renders your component with the new frame value, automatically producing the next image in your video sequence.

content.content:
# Remotion Rendering Overview

Based on the provided documentation, here's how Remotion rendering works:

## Primary Rendering Methods

**Remotion Studio** offers a GUI approach: users click the render button, configure settings, and confirm via the "Render video" button. The studio can also be deployed to a cloud server for team access.

**CLI Command** provides programmatic rendering:
```bash
npx remotion render HelloWorld
```

You can omit the composition name to trigger an interactive picker, or append an output path to override defaults.

## Additional Rendering Options

The documentation mentions several specialized approaches:

- **Server-Side Rendering (SSR)**: A "full-featured server-side rendering API" for backend rendering
- **AWS Lambda**: Distributed cloud rendering through Remotion Lambda
- **GitHub Actions**: Automated rendering workflows
- **Google Cloud Run**: An alpha integration planned for future enhancement

## Output Variants

Beyond standard video rendering, Remotion supports:
- Audio-only exports
- Image sequences (via `--sequence` flag)
- Still images
- GIF format
- Transparent videos for overlay creation

## Key Workflow

The document emphasizes flexibility: choose your environment (local studio, CLI, or cloud), configure composition and output preferences, then initiate rendering. The specific parameters (codec, format, resolution) aren't detailed in this excerpt, but are referenced as available options.

content.content:
# Remotion's Layout Utilities for Dynamic Video Text

Remotion's `@remotion/layout-utils` package helps developers manage text that needs to fit within constrained spaces during video composition. The core tool is `fillTextBox()`, which "Calculate[s] whether the text exceeds the box and wraps within the container."

## How It Works

The function operates by accepting text fragments one at a time and measuring whether each addition fits within your specified constraints:

- **maxBoxWidth**: The horizontal pixel limit for your text area
- **maxLines**: The maximum number of lines allowed

As you add words via the `add()` method, the utility returns feedback indicating:
- Whether the word causes text to overflow the line count
- Whether it requires starting a fresh line

## Text Measurement Capabilities

The system accepts typography parameters matching CSS propertiesâ€”font-family, font-size, font-weight, and text-transform. It can also validate whether custom fonts properly loaded before measurement, preventing fallback font measurement errors.

## Key Limitation

Importantly, this utility "Only works in the browser, not in Node.js or Bun," so server-side rendering workflows cannot use it directly.

This approach enables creators to build responsive text layouts that adapt during video generation, ensuring compositions remain visually correct regardless of content length.

content.content:
# Remotion Composition Structure Guide

Based on the documentation, Remotion recommends organizing compositions as follows:

## Core Structure

Place compositions in `src/Root.tsx` using the `<Composition>` component, which defines "the component, width, height, fps and duration of a renderable video."

## Organization Strategies

**Grouping with Folders**: Use `<Folder>` components to organize related compositions hierarchically in the sidebar. Folder names are restricted to letters, numbers, and hyphens.

**Static vs. Dynamic Content**: 
- Use `<Composition>` for video content with specific frame duration and fps settings
- Use `<Still>` for single-frame images, which "does not require durationInFrames or fps"

## Dynamic Configuration

For data-driven videos, implement `calculateMetadata` functions that "make dimensions, duration, or props dynamic based on data." This function runs once before rendering and can fetch external data to override placeholder values.

## Props Management

Pass `defaultProps` with JSON-serializable values (supporting `Date`, `Map`, `Set`, and `staticFile()`). The documentation emphasizes using TypeScript `type` declarations rather than `interface` for type safety.

## Nested Compositions

Embed compositions within others using `<Sequence>` components with explicit width and height props.

content.content:
# Remotion Animation Fundamentals

Based on the provided documentation, here's what we know about Remotion's animation capabilities:

## Core Animation Requirement

"All animations MUST be driven by the `useCurrentFrame()` hook." Animations must be time-based using frame calculations rather than traditional CSS methods.

## Key Animation Pattern

The documentation demonstrates animation through frame interpolation:

```tsx
const frame = useCurrentFrame();
const { fps } = useVideoConfig();
const opacity = interpolate(frame, [0, 2 * fps], [0, 1], {
  extrapolateRight: "clamp",
});
```

This shows that developers should "Write animations in seconds and multiply them by the `fps` value" to convert time into frame ranges.

## Forbidden Approaches

The documentation explicitly prohibits:
- CSS transitions or animations ("will not render correctly")
- Tailwind animation class names ("will not render correctly")

# Remotion Animation Fundamentals

Based on the provided documentation, here's what we know about Remotion's animation capabilities:

## Core Animation Requirement

"All animations MUST be driven by the `useCurrentFrame()` hook." Animations must be time-based using frame calculations rather than traditional CSS methods.

## Key Animation Pattern

The documentation demonstrates animation through frame interpolation:

```tsx
const frame = useCurrentFrame();
const { fps } = useVideoConfig();
const opacity = interpolate(frame, [0, 2 * fps], [0, 1], {
  extrapolateRight: "clamp",
});
```

This shows that developers should "Write animations in seconds and multiply them by the `fps` value" to convert time into frame ranges.

## Forbidden Approaches

The documentation explicitly prohibits:
- CSS transitions or animations ("will not render correctly")
- Tailwind animation class names ("will not render correctly")

## Mentioned Animation Primitives

The content references `interpolate` as a core animation API, and the page title suggests `spring` and other primitives exist, though detailed implementations aren't shown in this excerpt.

content.content:
# How Transitions Work in Remotion

Remotion's `<TransitionSeries>` component manages scene transitions using two distinct mechanisms:

**Transitions** create visual effects (like crossfades or slides) between scenes. They overlap both scenes during the effect, which actually shortens the total timeline duration. For instance, two 60-frame sequences with a 15-frame transition results in 105 total frames instead of 120.

**Overlays** render effects on top of cut points without affecting timeline length. They can be any React component, such as light leaks or other visual effects.

As stated in the documentation: "Transitions overlap adjacent scenes, so the total composition length is **shorter** than the sum of all sequence durations."

## Available Transition Types

Five built-in transition effects are available through dedicated modules:

- `fade()` â€” crossfade between scenes
- `slide()` â€” directional sliding (supports from-left, from-right, from-top, from-bottom)
- `wipe()` â€” progressive reveal effect
- `flip()` â€” rotation-based transition
- `clockWipe()` â€” clock-style wipe pattern

Each transition requires two configuration props: a `presentation` parameter specifying the visual effect, and a `timing` parameter controlling speed using either `linearTiming()` for constant speed or `springTiming()` for organic, physics-based motion.

I've explored the project context and gathered the Remotion documentation. Here's what I know so far:

**Founder OS patterns:** Skills are markdown knowledge documents in `development/skills/<namespace>/`, commands orchestrate them from `development/commands/<namespace>/`. There's existing media infrastructure (`late-media` skill, `media-handler-agent`) for uploading to social platforms. The content pipeline flows: `ideate:draft` -> `social:compose` -> `social:post`.

**Remotion:** React-based video framework. Videos are React components driven by `useCurrentFrame()`. Compositions define metadata (dimensions, fps, duration). Rendering via CLI (`npx remotion render`), SSR API, or Lambda. Official skills package has 38 rule files covering animations, transitions, captions, fonts, 3D, charts, etc.

No visual questions ahead (this is all architecture/workflow), so skipping the visual companion. First clarifying question:

options.label: Social content factory

options.label: Marketing/sales assets
options.description: Product demos, testimonial compilations, explainer videos, pitch decks as video. Higher production quality, fewer videos, more polish.

content.text:
Great, I have a clear picture. Here's a summary of decisions so far:

- **Use case:** Full spectrum (social clips + polished marketing videos)
- **Project home:** Managed subdirectory, one-time scaffold
- **Official skills:** Bundle `@remotion/skills` as dependency, layer FOS workflows on top
- **Rendering:** Local-first with optional cloud (Lambda)
- **Templates:** Composition-per-template (React components with typed props)
- **TTS:** Phase 2 (launch with text overlays + music first)
- **Integration:** New `video` namespace with hooks into `social:compose --format=video`

Now let me propose approaches:

## Approach A: Template-Driven Pipeline (Recommended)

FOS scaffolds a managed Remotion project with a curated template library (React compositions). Each template exposes typed props (headlines, images, colors, timing). Commands generate props from user input + brand kit, render locally via CLI, and hand off to the media-handler-agent for upload.

**Pros:** Predictable output, fast renders, easy to add templates, leverages official `@remotion/skills` for low-level guidance.
**Cons:** Templates need upfront design work. Users are constrained to template variants unless they edit React.

## Approach B: Hybrid Template + Generative

Same template library as A, but adds a `video:create` command where Claude generates custom Remotion compositions on-the-fly for unique requests. Falls back to templates for common patterns.

**Pros:** Maximum flexibility â€” templates for speed, generative for creativity.
**Cons:** Generated compositions may have rendering bugs. Harder to quality-control. Higher token cost per video.

## Approach C: Scene Graph Abstraction

A JSON-based scene description layer sits between FOS and Remotion. Commands produce scene JSON, a universal renderer translates to Remotion components. Templates are JSON files, not React code.

**Pros:** Users never touch React. Easier to serialize/store video definitions in Notion/SQLite.
**Cons:** Adds an abstraction layer that limits access to Remotion's full power. More code to maintain. Harder to leverage `@remotion/skills` rules directly.

**My recommendation: Approach A** â€” template-driven pipeline. It's the most predictable, maintainable, and aligned with how Founder OS already works (structured skills/commands with typed inputs). Templates can be expanded over time, and the composition-per-template model maps directly to Remotion's architecture. Approach B's generative mode can be added as a phase 2 enhancement alongside TTS.

content.text:
Approach A confirmed. Let me present the design in sections. Starting with the architecture overview.

---

## Section 1: Architecture Overview

**Managed Remotion Project** lives at a configurable path (default: `~/.founder-os/video-studio/`). FOS scaffolds it once via `video:init`, installing Remotion + `@remotion/skills` + dependencies. The project contains:

```
~/.founder-os/video-studio/
  â”œâ”€â”€ src/
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ Root.tsx              # Composition registry
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ templates/            # One React component per template
  â”‚   â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ SocialReel.tsx
  â”‚   â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ ProductDemo.tsx
  â”‚   â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ Testimonial.tsx
  â”‚   â”‚   â””â”€â”€ ...
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ components/           # Shared building blocks (TextOverlay, Logo, etc.)
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ hooks/                # Custom hooks (useBrandKit, useAsset)
  â”‚   â””â”€â”€ lib/                  # Utilities (timing helpers, color utils)
  â”œâ”€â”€ public/                   # Static assets (music, SFX, stock)
  â”œâ”€â”€ assets/                   # User assets (logos, fonts, images)
  â”œâ”€â”€ remotion.config.ts
  â”œâ”€â”€ package.json
  â””â”€â”€ tsconfig.json
```

**Data flow:**
1. User runs `/founder-os:video:generate --template=social-reel --script="..."`
2. FOS skill loads brand kit + resolves assets
3. Skill generates typed input props JSON for the template composition
4. FOS calls `npx remotion render <composition-id> --props=<props.json> --output=<path>`
5. Rendered video feeds into media-handler-agent for platform upload (if `--post` flag used)

**Key dependency:** `@remotion/skills` is installed in the managed project. FOS skills reference the official rules for Remotion-specific guidance (animations, transitions, fonts, captions), while FOS adds the workflow layer (brand kit, content pipeline, template selection).

Does this architecture look right so far?

## Section 2: Command Structure

New `video` namespace under `/founder-os:video:*`:

| Command | Purpose | Key Args |
|---------|---------|----------|
| `video:init` | One-time scaffold of managed Remotion project | `--path`, `--skip-templates` |
| `video:generate` | Generate a video from template + content | `--template`, `--script`, `--aspect`, `--duration`, `--post` |
| `video:preview` | Render still frames at key moments before full render | `--template`, `--frames=0,30,60` |
| `video:render` | Re-render an existing composition with updated props | `--composition`, `--props`, `--format` |
| `video:templates` | List available templates with descriptions and previews | `--category` |
| `video:brand` | Manage brand kit (colors, fonts, logos, music) | `--set`, `--show`, `--import` |

**Pipeline hook:** `social:compose` gains `--format=video` which delegates to `video:generate`, then passes the output path to the existing media-handler-agent upload flow.

**Preflight checks** for `video:*` commands:
- Node.js >= 18
- Remotion project exists (prompt `video:init` if not)
- ffmpeg available (for audio/video processing)
- Sufficient disk space for renders

Does this command structure work?

## Section 3: Template System

Each template is a React composition with a typed props interface. Templates ship with the managed project and can be extended by the user.

**Starter template library (8 templates across both use cases):**

| Template | Category | Aspect | Description |
|----------|----------|--------|-------------|
| `social-reel` | Social | 9:16 | Animated text slides with transitions, background music, brand colors |
| `social-quote` | Social | 1:1 | Quote card with animated text reveal, author attribution, logo |
| `social-listicle` | Social | 9:16 | Numbered tips/points with slide transitions |
| `social-before-after` | Social | 9:16 | Split-screen comparison with wipe transition |
| `product-demo` | Marketing | 16:9 | Screen recording frame + callouts + intro/outro |
| `testimonial` | Marketing | 16:9 | Customer quote with photo, company logo, animated text |
| `explainer` | Marketing | 16:9 | Section-based with icons, text animations, branded lower thirds |
| `pitch-highlight` | Marketing | 16:9 | Key metrics/stats with number animations, logo, CTA |

**Props contract example** (every template follows this pattern):

```typescript
interface SocialReelProps {
  slides: Array<{ headline: string; body?: string; image?: string }>;
  brandKit: { primaryColor: string; font: string; logoPath: string };
  music?: string;        // path to background audio
  durationPerSlide?: number; // seconds, default 3
  transition?: 'fade' | 'slide' | 'wipe'; // default 'slide'
}
```

**Template selection logic** (in the `video-templates` skill):
1. If `--template` specified, use it directly
2. Otherwise, match based on content type: script length, number of sections, presence of quotes/stats
3. Suggest template with rationale, let user confirm

**Brand kit resolution:** Templates pull from `assets/brand-kit.json` in the managed project. The `video:brand` command populates this from the founder's existing brand context (colors, fonts, logos already stored in FOS business context files).

Does this template system make sense?

## Section 4: Skill Structure & Official Skills Integration

**FOS skill files** (markdown knowledge documents following existing patterns):

| Skill | Path | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `video-generation` | `skills/video/video-generation/SKILL.md` | Orchestrates the generate workflow: parse script, select template, resolve assets, build props JSON, invoke render |
| `video-templates` | `skills/video/video-templates/SKILL.md` | Template selection algorithm, prop mapping rules, how to match content to templates |
| `video-rendering` | `skills/video/video-rendering/SKILL.md` | Render pipeline: local CLI invocation, output format selection, preview frame extraction, error recovery |
| `brand-kit` | `skills/video/brand-kit/SKILL.md` | Brand asset resolution, color/font/logo mapping, fallback defaults |

**How `@remotion/skills` integrates:**

The official package is installed in the managed Remotion project (not in the FOS plugin). FOS skills reference it as the authoritative source for low-level Remotion guidance:

```
# In video-generation SKILL.md:
When generating composition code or modifying templates, the AI agent 
has access to @remotion/skills rules in the Remotion project at 
{videoStudioPath}/node_modules/@remotion/skills/. These 38 rule files 
cover animations, transitions, fonts, captions, timing, etc.

FOS skills handle WHAT to build (template selection, content mapping, 
brand application). @remotion/skills handles HOW to build it 
(Remotion API best practices, animation patterns, rendering rules).
```

**Layered responsibility:**
- **FOS skills** = founder workflow (content brief -> template -> props -> render -> upload)
- **@remotion/skills** = Remotion best practices (use `interpolate` not CSS transitions, `useCurrentFrame()` for all animation, `<TransitionSeries>` for scene transitions)

The agent reads both when working on video tasks â€” FOS skills for the workflow, official skills for Remotion-specific implementation guidance.

Does this skill layering approach work?

## Section 5: Rendering Pipeline & Output

**Local render flow (default):**

```
video:generate
  â”œâ”€ 1. Build props JSON from script + brand kit + assets
  â”œâ”€ 2. Validate assets exist (images, fonts, music)
  â”œâ”€ 3. Preview: render 3 still frames (start, middle, end)
  â”‚     â””â”€ npx remotion still <comp> --frame=N --output=preview-N.png
  â”œâ”€ 4. Show previews to user, confirm before full render
  â”œâ”€ 5. Full render:
  â”‚     â””â”€ npx remotion render <comp> --props=props.json \
  â”‚          --output=output.mp4 --codec=h264
  â””â”€ 6. Report: file path, duration, size, resolution
```

**Output format presets:**

| Preset | Resolution | Aspect | FPS | Codec | Use case |
|--------|-----------|--------|-----|-------|----------|
| `reel` | 1080x1920 | 9:16 | 30 | H.264 | Instagram/TikTok reels |
| `story` | 1080x1920 | 9:16 | 30 | H.264 | Stories (15s max) |
| `square` | 1080x1080 | 1:1 | 30 | H.264 | Feed posts, LinkedIn |
| `landscape` | 1920x1080 | 16:9 | 30 | H.264 | YouTube, presentations |
| `gif` | 800x800 | 1:1 | 15 | GIF | Lightweight social clips |

**Cloud render option (phase 2):**
- Remotion Lambda integration via `--cloud` flag
- Requires one-time AWS setup (`video:cloud-init`)
- Parallel rendering for batch jobs (e.g., render 5 platform variants at once)
- Cost estimation shown before confirming cloud render

**Preview workflow** is key for the founder experience â€” seeing 3 still frames takes ~5 seconds vs a full render that could take 30-120 seconds. Users confirm before committing to the full render, avoiding wasted time on wrong props.

**Error recovery:**
- Missing font: fall back to system font, warn user
- Missing image: render with placeholder, warn user
- Render crash: capture Remotion error output, suggest fix (usually a prop type mismatch or missing asset)
- Disk space: check before render, warn if < 500MB free

Does this rendering pipeline look right?

## Section 6: Asset Management & Brand Kit

**Asset storage** in the managed Remotion project:

```
~/.founder-os/video-studio/
  â”œâ”€â”€ assets/
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ brand-kit.json        # Resolved brand config
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ logos/                 # Company logos (PNG/SVG)
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ fonts/                # Custom fonts (.woff2, .ttf)
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ music/                # Background tracks
  â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ sfx/                  # Sound effects
  â”‚   â””â”€â”€ images/               # Reusable images (headshots, product shots)
  â””â”€â”€ public/
      â””â”€â”€ stock/                # Bundled stock assets (subtle backgrounds, textures)
```

**Brand kit resolution chain:**
1. Check `assets/brand-kit.json` in the Remotion project (cached, fastest)
2. If missing/stale, pull from FOS business context (`_infrastructure/context/active/`)
3. If no business context, prompt user via `video:brand --set`

**brand-kit.json structure:**

```json
{
  "company": "Acme Inc",
  "colors": {
    "primary": "#2563EB",
    "secondary": "#1E40AF",
    "accent": "#F59E0B",
    "background": "#0F172A",
    "text": "#F8FAFC"
  },
  "fonts": {
    "heading": "Inter-Bold.woff2",
    "body": "Inter-Regular.woff2"
  },
  "logo": {
    "primary": "logos/logo-light.svg",
    "dark": "logos/logo-dark.svg",
    "icon": "logos/icon.svg"
  },
  "music": {
    "default": "music/upbeat-corporate.mp3",
    "calm": "music/ambient-soft.mp3"
  }
}
```

**Asset validation** (in the `video-rendering` skill):
- Before render: verify all referenced files exist, are valid formats, and are reasonable sizes
- Fonts: must be `.woff2` or `.ttf`, auto-register via Remotion's `loadFont()` from `@remotion/fonts`
- Images: must be PNG, JPG, SVG, or WebP. Warn if resolution is too low for output format
- Audio: must be MP3, WAV, or AAC. Warn if duration mismatches video duration

**Stock assets bundled with scaffolding:**
- 3-4 royalty-free background music tracks (upbeat, calm, energetic, minimal)
- Subtle gradient/texture backgrounds
- Basic transition SFX (whoosh, pop)

Does this asset management approach work?

## Section 7: Content Pipeline Integration & Phasing

**Integration with existing content pipeline:**

```
ideate:draft â”€â”€> social:compose â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€> text post â”€â”€> social:post
                                  â”‚
                                  â””â”€â”€> --format=video
                                       â”‚
                                       â”œâ”€ video:generate (template + props)
                                       â”œâ”€ video:preview (confirm stills)
                                       â”œâ”€ video:render (full render)
                                       â””â”€ media-handler-agent (upload)
                                            â””â”€â”€> social:post (publish)
```

**The `social:compose --format=video` bridge:**
- Takes the content brief from `ideate:draft` (headlines, body text, key points)
- Selects a video template based on content type and target platform
- Passes to `video:generate` with the brief as script input
- Returns the rendered video path for `social:post` to upload

**Standalone usage** also works â€” users can call `video:generate` directly without going through the social pipeline, for marketing videos, presentations, or internal content.

---

**Phasing:**

| Phase | Scope | Dependencies |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| **Phase 1** | `video:init`, `video:generate`, `video:preview`, `video:render`, `video:templates`, `video:brand`. 4 social templates + 4 marketing templates. Local rendering only. Brand kit integration. | Remotion project scaffolding, template React components |
| **Phase 2** | TTS/voiceover integration (ElevenLabs or OpenAI TTS). Auto-caption generation using Remotion's `@remotion/install-whisper-cpp` + subtitle rules. `social:compose --format=video` bridge. | TTS provider API key, Whisper model |
| **Phase 3** | Cloud rendering via Remotion Lambda (`video:cloud-init`). Batch rendering (multi-platform variants). Custom template creation command (`video:template:create`). | AWS account, Lambda setup |

**What ships in Phase 1 (MVP):**
- One-time `video:init` scaffolding
- 8 starter templates covering social + marketing
- Brand kit management
- Preview-before-render workflow
- Local H.264/GIF rendering
- 5 output presets (reel, story, square, landscape, gif)
- FOS skill files referencing `@remotion/skills` for Remotion guidance

That covers the full design. Does this phasing and integration approach look good?

---
description: Create and publish content to one or more social media platforms
argument-hint: '"Post text" --platforms=linkedin,x [--from=file|url] [--media=path] [--schedule=time] [--draft] [--thread] [--team] [--audience=hint] [--tone=professional|friendly|casual|authoritative|conversational] [--format=table|json|markdown]'
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash"]
execution-mode: background
result-format: summary
---

# social:post

Create and publish content to one or more platforms via Late.dev.

## Skills

Read these skill files before proceeding:

1. Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/late-skills/late-common/SKILL.md` â€” auth, errors, conventions
2. Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/late-skills/late-publish/SKILL.md` â€” publishing patterns
3. Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/social/platform-adaptation/SKILL.md` â€” per-platform rules
4. Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/humanize-content/SKILL.md`
5. Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/humanize-content/references/social-humanization.md`
6. If `--schedule` provided: Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/social/posting-cadence/SKILL.md`
7. If `--media` provided: Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/late-skills/late-media/SKILL.md`

## Arguments

| Argument | Required | Description |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| `text` | Yes (unless `--from`) | Post text (positional) |
| `--platforms` | Yes | Comma-separated target platforms (e.g., `linkedin,x`) |
| `--from` | No | Source file path or Notion page URL |
| `--media` | No | Comma-separated file paths for images/videos/documents |
| `--schedule` | No | Schedule for future time (natural language or ISO 8601) |
| `--draft` | No | Save as draft without publishing |
| `--thread` | No | Enable thread mode (X threads, LinkedIn carousel) |
| `--team` | No | Activate full agent pipeline |
| `--audience` | No | Target audience hint for content adaptation |
| `--tone` | No | Humanization tone: professional (default), friendly, casual, authoritative, conversational |
| `--format` | No | Output format: table (default), json, markdown |

## Business Context (Optional)

Check `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/context/active/` for `.md` files. If present, read them to personalize tone, voice, and brand.

## Preflight Check

Run `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/preflight/SKILL.md` for namespace `social`.
- Required: `late` (validate `$LATE_API_KEY` + probe `late-tool.mjs --validate-only`)
- Optional: `notion` (for Content DB logging), `filesystem` (for `--from` file source)

**Interim check** (until preflight ships):
```bash
node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/late-tool.mjs --validate-only
```
If failed: show fix instructions from `_infrastructure/preflight/references/fix-messages.md` > `late`.

## Step 0: Memory Context

Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/_infrastructure/memory/context-injection/SKILL.md`.
Query memory store for: `social posting`, `linkedin`, `content publishing`, user's brand voice.
Inject top 5 relevant memories.

## Intelligence: Apply Learned Patterns

Check for learned optimizations from past `social:post` runs.

## Phase 1/3: Content Preparation

Display: `Phase 1/3: Preparing content...`

1. **Parse source content**:
   - If `--from` is a file path: read the file
   - If `--from` is a Notion URL: fetch page content via `notion-tool.mjs`
   - If positional `text`: use directly
2. **Detect content type**: short post, long-form, listicle, thread-candidate
3. **Apply audience hint** if `--audience` provided

## Phase 2/3: Platform Adaptation

Display: `Phase 2/3: Adapting for platforms...`

file.content:
---
name: template-engine
description: "Activates when creating content from templates. Provides template selection logic, combination strategies, and performance-based ranking."
globs: ["commands/social/compose.md", "commands/social/ab-test.md"]
---

# Template Engine

Template selection, combination, and performance-based ranking for social content generation. Used by the `social:compose` and `social:ab-test` commands.

## 1. Template Selection Algorithm

**Step 1: Load the template index**

Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/social/templates/_index.yaml` to get the full list of available templates with their metadata.

**Step 2: Filter by platform**

Use the `platform_affinity` section in `_index.yaml` to narrow the candidate list to templates suited for the target platform (e.g., `linkedin`, `twitter`).

**Step 3: Filter by technique (if specified)**

If the user passed `--technique <name>`, narrow candidates further to that technique group using the `techniques` section in `_index.yaml`. Skip this step if no technique is specified.

**Step 4: Score by topic relevance**

Score each remaining candidate's tags against the topic keywords using fuzzy matching. Higher overlap = higher base score.

**Step 5: Apply performance bias (if data available)**

Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/social/templates/_performance.yaml`. If the file exists and is non-empty:
- Find technique scores for similar topic categories using `best_topics` lists in `technique_scores`.
- Boost templates from techniques with higher win rates.
- Only apply this bias after a minimum of 3 A/B tests per technique. Techniques with fewer than 3 tests are excluded from the boost calculation.

**Step 6: Rank and select candidates**

Rank all scored candidates and select the top N (N determined by command context, e.g., number of variations requested for A/B testing).

**Step 7: Load selected templates**

Read each selected template's full content from `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/social/templates/{name}.md`.

## 2. Combination Strategies

### Technique Stacking (2â€“3 techniques in one post)

1. Read the "Why It Works" section from each selected template to understand the psychological mechanism.
2. Check compatibility before combining:
   - Compatible example: Anaphora + List (repetition reinforces structure).
   - Potentially clashing example: Reversal + Misdirection (both redirect attention; may dilute each other).
3. Merge structures: identify the strongest structural element from each technique and combine them.
4. Maintain a consistent tone throughout the merged post.

### Section Assembly (hook + body + closer)

1. Pick a hook template from `combinations.hooks` in `_index.yaml`.
2. Pick a body template from `combinations.bodies`.
3. Pick a closer template from `combinations.closers`.
4. Adapt transitions between sections to ensure smooth, natural flow.

    33â†’|----------|--------|-------|-------|

    35â†’| X/Twitter | 4 images max OR 1 video | MP4/MOV | â€” |

file.content:
---
name: late-media
description: "Late.dev media upload workflow with presigned URLs, validation, and platform-specific constraints"
---

# Late.dev Media Upload Workflow

## Upload Flow

1. **Validate** file exists, check type/size against platform limits
2. **Validate content-type** matches actual file magic bytes (security)
3. **Get presigned URL**: `late-tool.mjs media presign --filename=photo.jpg --content-type=image/jpeg`
4. **Upload** file to presigned URL via PUT (handled internally)
5. **Reference** `publicUrl` in post's `mediaItems` array

```bash
# Step 1: Get presigned URL
node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/late-tool.mjs media presign \
  --filename=photo.jpg --content-type=image/jpeg
# Returns: { "publicUrl": "https://...", "uploadUrl": "https://..." }
# Note: uploadUrl is internal only â€” never log it

# Step 2: Reference in post
node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/late-tool.mjs posts create \
  --accounts='["acc_123"]' \
  --text="Check this out!" \
  --media='[{"publicUrl":"https://...","type":"image"}]'
```

## Platform Constraints

| Platform | Images | Video | Other |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| LinkedIn | JPEG/PNG/GIF (multiple) | MP4/MOV | PDF docs (100MB, 300pp max) |
| X/Twitter | 4 images max OR 1 video | MP4/MOV | â€” |

## Security

- Presigned URLs are bearer tokens â€” **never persist to logs or DB**
- Use immediately after creation (TTL ~15 minutes, Late.dev-controlled). If the API allows configuring TTL, request 5 minutes maximum to minimize exposure window
- Validate content-type matches file magic bytes before upload
- Max 5GB per file

# [20] Remotion Skill Design

> **Status:** Complete â€” awaiting review
> **Date:** 2026-03-19
> **Approach:** Template-Driven Pipeline (Approach A)

## 1. Overview

Claude Code skill for short-form and marketing video creation with Remotion. Enables founders to generate, preview, and render branded video content directly from Claude Code using the Remotion framework â€” React components driven by frame-based animation.

**Target use cases:**
- **Social content factory:** Reels, stories, quote cards, listicles for LinkedIn/X/Instagram
- **Marketing assets:** Product demos, testimonials, explainer videos, pitch highlights

**Core principle:** Composition-per-template. Each video type is a React composition with typed props. FOS generates the props from user input + brand kit, renders locally via Remotion CLI, and optionally uploads via the existing media-handler-agent pipeline.

## 2. Architecture

### 2.1 Managed Remotion Project

A self-contained Remotion project scaffolded once via `video:init` at a configurable path (default: `~/.founder-os/video-studio/`). The user never needs to touch it directly.
